1:
The Chronicler, having traced the tribe of Judah down to David, now opens the king's own house, numbering first the sons born to him in Hebron: the firstborn Amnon of Ahinoam the Jezreelitess, and the second, called here Daniel, of Abigail the Carmelitess. The reformed John Gill notes that these six Hebron-born sons are reckoned in the same order as in the older record, and that the second son, named Daniel here, goes by the name Chileab in 2 Sam. 3; the man simply bore two names. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree that the difference cannot well have arisen through a copyist's error but is best explained by supposing this son had two different names, and they note the grammatical oddity that the phrase 'a second' stands here without the article that governs all the other numbers.

Over the reason for the double name the witnesses gather traditions. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum, which says the second son was 'called Chileab because he was in every respect like to his father,' reading the name as ke-le-ab, like to the father; and he adds Jarchi's sharper explanation, that because David took Abigail so soon after Nabal's death it could not at first be told whether the child were David's or Nabal's, so David named him Daniel, 'God is my judge — I have not fathered another man's child,' and Chileab, 'he who is like to the father,' the boy's very features silencing the suspicion. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the plain cross-references — Amnon, whose story is told in 2 Sam. 13, Ahinoam named in 1 Sam. 25:43, and Abigail whose history fills 1 Sam. 25.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the bare list into praise of the house it records. Of all the families of Israel, he says, none were so illustrious as the family of David, for from this family, as concerning the flesh, Christ came. David had many sons, and wrote as he thought, that happy is the man whose quiver is full of them; yet some were a grief to him — Amnon, Absalom, and Adonijah — and none imitated his piety save Solomon, who came far short of it. Henry marks it a mercy that the son Bathsheba bore he named Nathan, in honour of the prophet who had reproved him and brought him to repentance, esteeming as his best friend the man who had dealt faithfully with him; and from this Nathan, he observes, our Lord Jesus descended, as appears in Luke 3:31. So the witnesses read the chapter's first verse as the doorway into the pedigree no other nation could show, the line that runs from David to the Messiah.
5:
The scene crosses from Hebron to the new capital, and the register opens the Jerusalem-born sons with the four children of one mother: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon, of Bathshua the daughter of Ammiel. The reformed John Gill observes that in all nine sons were born to David in Jerusalem, though only seven are named in the parallel place, and he draws attention to the mother's name: Bathshua here is the Bathsheba of the history, and her father Ammiel is the Eliam of the older record, the two forms being names of much the same signification, differently pronounced.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke confirms the spelling and explains it as a copyist's exchange of letters, the Hebrew Bath-shua and Bath-sheba differing only by a single character wrongly set for its like. He notes too the order of the four: Solomon is placed last, though he was the eldest of these sons of Bathsheba, because it was from him that the genealogy was to be continued, and the historian keeps the line-bearer for the end. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the plain force of the number: all four were David's children by her, as the text positively affirms, so that when Solomon is called her 'only son' in Prov. 4:3 the word must mean that she loved him as if he were her only one, not that she bore no other.

The witnesses find in this verse the quiet mercy that runs beneath the whole pedigree. Bathsheba's name, freighted with David's darkest sin, stands here without reproach at the head of the Jerusalem sons, and among her children is Solomon, the chosen heir, and Nathan, from whom, as the genealogists know, the other line of the Messiah's descent would run. The very woman whose story had brought the prophet Nathan to David's door with a word of judgment is here the mother of the royal seed; and the historian, naming her sons in order and keeping Solomon for the last, shows how God carried his covenant forward not around David's failure but, astonishingly, through the house that failure had touched. Grace, not merit, sets the line in motion at Jerusalem.
9:
The historian draws a line beneath David's sons: 'These were all the sons of David, beside the sons of the concubines, and Tamar their sister.' The reformed John Gill takes the summary at its face — these were all his sons by his wives — the concubines' children being set apart and, as the register shows, left unnamed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott enlarges on both the concubines and the sister. David's concubines, the pilagshim, are mentioned several times in the history of his reign, yet their sons are named nowhere but by this bare notice; however repugnant to modern feeling, he observes, it was part of the state of an Oriental king to keep such a household. And Tamar is named, he says, not as David's only daughter but as the one whose unhappy fate had made her famous, the sister whose wrong is told in 2 Sam. 13.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks the poignancy of that single name: Tamar is the only daughter of David whose name is on record, and yet the history says plainly that he had both sons and daughters, as at 2 Sam. 5:13. The many are passed over in silence; the one is remembered for her sorrow. Ellicott adds a scholar's judgment on the list as a whole — that the Chronicler's roll of David's sons, differing as it does from the parallels in form and in several names, and alone giving the numbers four and nine, was probably not drawn from the book of Samuel at all, but from an independent register.

The witnesses read the verse as a deliberate narrowing and a sober honesty. The concubines' sons are acknowledged but not honoured with names, for the line of promise does not run through them; the roll is being cleared of all but the sons who matter to the descent. Yet the historian will not tidy away the house's griefs: he names Tamar, and in naming her lets the shadow of Amnon's crime and Absalom's revenge fall once more across the pedigree. Gill, Clarke, and Ellicott together leave the verse as the close of David's family and the threshold of David's throne — every son counted, the outsiders set aside, one wronged daughter remembered, and the way now open for the register to follow Solomon alone down the line of the kings.
10:
Here the register leaves the sons of David for the throne of David: 'And Solomon's son was Rehoboam, Abia his son, Asa his son, Jehoshaphat his son.' The reformed John Gill notes simply that from this verse to the end of the fourteenth, David's successors are reckoned in the order of their reigns, down to Josiah and his sons. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the meaning locked in the royal names, each a little confession of faith: Rehoboam, 'the kinsman hath enlarged'; Abijah, 'Iah is father'; Asa, 'healer'; Jehoshaphat, 'Iahweh judgeth' — the throne itself spelling out the God whose vicegerents these kings were meant to be.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse the doorway to the whole line and reads the line as the recompense of David's piety. Of David's many sons, he observes, Scripture follows only the descendants of Solomon here and of Nathan in Luke 3; the rest had the honour to be sons of David, but these alone the honour to be related to the Messiah — the sons of Nathan his fathers as man, the sons of Solomon his predecessors as king. He marvels that seldom has a crown gone in a direct line from father to son for so many descents together as it does here, and he traces the reason to God's reward of David's faithfulness. Yet he notes the ominous turn to come: about the time of the captivity the lineal descent would be interrupted, the crown passing from brother to brother and from nephew to uncle, a presage of the eclipsing of that house's glory.

Henry's eye runs even to the chapter's last name, Anani, of whom Bishop Patrick reports that the Targum adds, 'He is the king Messiah, who is to be revealed,' the Jewish writers reasoning from the 'clouds' of Dan. 7:13; and though Henry calls the ground of it far-fetched, he takes it as evidence that their minds were always full of the thought of the Messiah, expecting his coming not long after the days of Zerubbabel. So the witnesses open the roll of the kings with a double note that will govern all that follows: an unbroken succession from Solomon that testifies to God's covenant faithfulness, and, hidden in the very last name of the line, the hope of the King toward whom the whole descending list is bent.
15:
With the sons of Josiah the orderly line at last gives way: 'the firstborn Johanan, the second Jehoiakim, the third Zedekiah, the fourth Shallum.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the change at once — the regular succession by primogeniture ceases with Josiah — and works through the four names with care. Johanan, the firstborn, 'Iahweh bestowed,' never came to the throne and may have died early; he is not to be confused with Jehoahaz, who was younger than Jehoiakim and so could not have been Josiah's firstborn. The other three, Ellicott shows, are known under other names in the history: Shallum is the Jehoahaz who reigned first and briefly, as 2 Kings 23:30 and Jer. 22:11 attest; Jehoiakim is the Eliakim of 2 Kings 23:34; and Zedekiah is the Mattaniah of 2 Kings 24:17.

Ellicott then explains why the order here follows neither strict age nor the actual sequence of reigns. Were it by seniority, the order would be Jehoiakim, Shallum, Zedekiah; were it by the succession on the throne, Shallum, Jehoiakim, Zedekiah. The historian's arrangement, he suggests, was swayed by two facts — that Jehoiakim and Zedekiah each reigned eleven years while Shallum reigned but three months, and that Shallum and Zedekiah were full brothers, both sons of Hamutal, whereas Jehoiakim was born of another wife, Zebudah. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds only the identification that most agree upon, that Shallum is the same who is called Jehoahaz in 2 Kings 23:30.

The witnesses read the tangle itself as the mark of a house in decline. Where fifteen kings had descended one from another in a line that testified to God's covenant, now four sons of one father must be numbered together, none of them succeeding by simple birthright, all of them tossed up and cast down by Egypt and Babylon. The very disorder of the list — the shifting names, the broken order, the reigns of months where once were reigns of decades — is the genealogy's way of showing the glory departing from David's house, as the prophets had warned. The Chronicler sets the four sons of Josiah side by side, and the reader sees the throne that had stood so long beginning to totter toward the captivity.
16:
A perplexing name follows: 'the sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah his son, Zedekiah his son.' The trouble is that the Zedekiah who reigned last over Judah was not Jehoiakim's son but Josiah's, the uncle of Jeconiah, so the witnesses divide over who this Zedekiah is. The reformed John Gill judges that he cannot be the famous last king at all — that Zedekiah was Jeconiah's uncle, not his brother — and takes the name rather for another Zedekiah, otherwise unmentioned, a true son of Jehoiakim who never came to notice. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees flatly: 'this was another Zedekiah,' and turns to muse on the marvel that the crown had run in a direct line for so many generations, until about the captivity the descent was interrupted and the throne passed from a nephew to an uncle, a presage of the glory departing from David's house.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes the opposite road. If this be the same Zedekiah who was Judah's last king, then the word 'son' must here mean 'successor,' for it is certain that Zedekiah followed Jeconiah on the throne, though he was the son of Josiah and not of Jehoiakim. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs both and comes down with Gill and Wesley: 'his son' bears its natural sense all through this list, and there is really nothing against the plain statement that Jeconiah had a son named Zedekiah, called after his great-uncle, who, like Josiah's firstborn Johanan, never reached the throne and so is heard of nowhere else. Ellicott adds the identification of the elder name, that Jeconiah is the Jehoiachin of the history and the Coniah of Jer. 22:24, carried captive to Babylon by Nebuchadnezzar, as 2 Kings 24:15 records.

So the debate is genuine: does 'Zedekiah his son' name the last king of Judah, listed as Jeconiah's royal successor, or a younger and forgotten Zedekiah who bore the name but never wore the crown? Clarke reads 'son' as 'successor' to save the identity; Gill, Wesley, and Ellicott read 'son' in its plain sense and multiply the Zedekiahs. The witnesses leave the knot half-tied, but their preference is clear — better a second Zedekiah lost to history than a strained sense forced on a word the register uses everywhere else in its ordinary meaning.
17:
The captivity now stamps itself upon the very names of the line: 'the sons of Jeconiah; Assir, Salathiel his son.' The reformed John Gill first clears away a difficulty of another kind — that Jeconiah had been pronounced childless in Jer. 22:30 — explaining that the word cannot mean he had no children in any sense, but that none of his seed should succeed him on the throne, as the prophet himself goes on to interpret it. Over the name Assir, however, the witnesses divide. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke doubts that Assir was a person at all: since the Hebrew word means 'a prisoner,' it may be an epithet of Jeconiah himself, who was so long a captive in Babylon, and Salathiel was in truth the son not of Assir but of Jeconiah, as Matt. 1:12 attests.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets out the case in full. Assir means 'prisoner' or 'captive,' as the word is used in Isa. 10:2; the verse may therefore be rendered, 'the sons of Jeconiah when captive — Shealtiel his son.' This accords with the Masoretic pointing, which joins the word to Jeconiah, and it accounts for Zedekiah's being named apart in the verse before, separated from the sons born to Jeconiah in his captivity. Ellicott grants that 'Jeconiah the captive' would strictly require the article, but insists the writer meant not that but 'Jeconiah when in captivity'; and he notes that the Talmudic tract Sanhedrin does give 'Assir his son,' while the Seder Olam omits Assir, who is likewise absent from the genealogy of our Lord. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads it the same way — 'of Jechoniah the captive' — added, he says, to show that Jeconiah begat his son while a prisoner in Babylon; and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Luther and others long since took Assir not as a name but as the appellative 'prisoner' set in apposition to Jeconiah.

The disagreement is thus sharply drawn: is Assir the name of a son of Jeconiah, or the word 'captive' describing Jeconiah as he begot Shealtiel in exile? The weight of the witnesses falls on the second reading — Clarke, Ellicott, Wesley, and the tradition Keil and Delitzsch report all hearing in the word not a man but the mark of the chains, so that the genealogy of the kings carries, at its lowest point, the very name of the bondage into which the house of David had fallen.
18:
Six more names are set down — 'Malchiram also, and Pedaiah, and Shenazar, Jecamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah' — and at once a question of relation arises. As the Hebrew stands, the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes, these six, arranged as two groups of three, are sons of Jeconiah and brothers of Shealtiel. But the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the older opinion of Calmet, that the verse should be read, 'and the sons of Salathiel were Malchiram and Pedaiah,' making them Shealtiel's sons rather than his brothers; and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley likewise judges the sentence short and imperfect, as is frequent in Hebrew, and supplies, 'the sons also of Salathiel were Malchiram and Pedaiah,' the very reading, he adds, from which men gather that Zerubbabel is called the son of Pedaiah in the next verse and the son — that is, the grandson — of Salathiel in Matt. 1:12.

Ellicott lingers over two of the names for what they reveal of the exile. Shenazar he takes for a compound Babylonian name, like Belteshazzar in Dan. 1:7, its latter part meaning 'protect' and its first perhaps 'Sin,' the moon-god — so that 'Sin protect' may well have been the name given to this Jewish prince at the court of Babylon, as Daniel and his companions received idolatrous names there. This, he observes, lends support to reading Assir in the previous verse as 'captive.' Hoshama, by contrast, is a contraction of Jehoshama, 'Iahweh hath heard,' shortened as Coniah was shortened from Jeconiah — a covenant name surviving beside the pagan one.

The witnesses read the verse as the register's descent into the strange soil of Babylon. Whether these six are the sons or the brothers of Shealtiel, they are the grandchildren of a captive king, and their very names tell the story of the exile — one prince called after the moon-god of his conquerors, another still bearing witness that 'the LORD hath heard.' Keil and Delitzsch, taking the block of names from here to the chapter's close, treat them as the plain record of the families that carried David's blood through the captivity and out the other side; and the mingling of Babylonian and Hebrew names in a single household is itself a picture of a people held in a foreign land yet not letting go the God of their fathers.
19:
The most famous name of the post-exilic line raises the chapter's knottiest tangle: 'the sons of Pedaiah were Zerubbabel and Shimei.' For everywhere else — in Haggai, in Ezra 3:2, in Matt. 1:12 — Zerubbabel is called the son of Shealtiel, not of Pedaiah. The reformed John Gill offers three ways to reconcile it: that Pedaiah was himself a son of Salathiel, so that Zerubbabel his grandson might be called Salathiel's son; or that Salathiel, dying childless, adopted his brother's son Zerubbabel and made him his heir; or that Salathiel died without issue and Pedaiah, by the law of the levirate, raised up seed to him, so that Zerubbabel was Pedaiah's son by nature and Salathiel's by law. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley holds the plain version of the same — Zerubbabel the proper son of Pedaiah and the grandson of Salathiel.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports a bolder cut: Houbigant would strike out the words 'the sons of Pedaiah' altogether, since Pedaiah is wanting in the Arabic and Syriac, so that Zerubbabel would stand as the son of Salathiel, in agreement with Matt. 1:12. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott declines to emend, holding that the Hebrew, peculiar as it is, makes Zerubbabel the son of Pedaiah and the nephew of Shealtiel; if, for reasons unknown, Zerubbabel became the adopted son and heir of his uncle, then all the seemingly discordant passages are reconciled, and the Chronicler's is the more exact. Ellicott names Zerubbabel the famous prince who led the first colony of exiles home under Cyrus, his name meaning 'born at Babel,' his father fitly called Pedaiah, 'Iah hath redeemed'; and of the children of Zerubbabel — Meshullam, Hananiah, and Shelomith their sister — he takes the naming of the sister to mark that the three were the offspring of one mother.

The debate is real and old: is Zerubbabel the son of Pedaiah, as the text says, or of Shealtiel, as the prophets and the Gospel say? Clarke's Houbigant would mend the text; Gill, Wesley, and Ellicott would rather harmonize it, by adoption or by the levirate, keeping the Chronicler's exact word and reading Shealtiel's fatherhood as legal or adoptive. The witnesses leave the reader with a Zerubbabel who is son of two fathers, and see in the very difficulty the sign of a house whose records were carefully kept even through the disorder of exile and return.
2:
The roll of the Hebron-born sons continues with the third and fourth: Absalom, the son of Maachah the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur, and Adonijah the son of Haggith. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the enumeration forward name by name, treating this verse as one link in the six-son list that the historian has drawn, without alteration of the text, from the record already given of David's reign. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the two royal sons and the shadows their names cast. Absalom he marks as David's favourite and rebellious son, whose revolt fills the long tragedy of 2 Sam. 15-19; and he notes a textual nicety, that where the common Hebrew reads 'to Absalom,' many manuscripts and all the old versions read simply 'Absalom.'

Ellicott preserves too the rabbinic wordplay on the name. Rabbi D. Kimchi, he reports, found in the form L-ABSHALOM an allusion to LO-ABSHALOM, 'not Absalom' — that is, no true 'father of peace' at all, but a rebel against his father and his king, the name turned inside out by the son's own treason. On Maachah and her Geshurite father Ellicott points back to the notice of Geshur already given at 1 Chron. 2:23, the princely house from which David took a wife. And Adonijah the son of Haggith he identifies as the prince who would have seized the succession, and who was put to death by Solomon, as the opening of the history in 1 Kings 1 and 1 Kings 2:19-25 relates.

The witnesses feel the weight the register quietly carries. Three of David's Hebron sons — Amnon, Absalom, Adonijah — came to violent or shameful ends, and here the second and third of that grievous trio stand side by side, the beautiful traitor and the ambitious pretender. Keil and Delitzsch's sober copying and Ellicott's philological care do not moralize, yet the reader who knows the story cannot number Absalom and Adonijah without remembering how nearly the house of David tore itself apart, and how the crown passed at last not to the eldest or the boldest but to Solomon, the son on whom God had set his choice. The genealogy names the rivals only to pass them by, bending, as it always bends, toward the one line of promise.
6:
The Jerusalem list lengthens with three more sons — Ibhar, and Elishama, and Eliphelet — and the witnesses take the occasion to face a small puzzle that runs across these verses: two of the names, Elishama and Eliphelet, will appear again among the sons numbered in v.8. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the difficulty and the two ways of meeting it: some think the repetition a mistake, but others suppose that two sons of these names died young, and that the next-born received the name of the deceased. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley holds firmly to the second view — David had two other sons called by the same names, probably by different wives, the earlier pair having died before the later were born, so that the names of the dead were given to the living to preserve their memory.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott resists the charge of error from another side. The recurrence of Elishama, 'God heareth,' and of Eliphelet, 'God is deliverance,' is no argument against the names here, for David may well have chosen names so expressive of his own faith and trust for the sons of different wives. Ellicott notes further that this Elishama is spelt Elishua in both parallel lists, and that Nogah, 'brightness,' like Eliphelet, is one of the names dropped from the record in Samuel — differences of copy and of selection, not marks of corruption.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the whole cluster in order, observing that thirteen sons in all were born to David in Jerusalem, that the same thirteen are enumerated again in 1 Chron. 14, and that the parallel in 2 Sam. 5:14-16 names only eleven, the last two omitted. Some of the names, they add, shift slightly in form between the lists through differences of pronunciation. So the witnesses divide gently over the doubled names — a scribe's slip to some, to others a father's deliberate renaming of sons lost in infancy — while agreeing that the register, for all its small variations, faithfully preserves the full number of the house that God was building toward its King.
11:
The descent of the kings runs on: 'Joram his son, Ahaziah his son, Joash his son.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, surveying the whole list from Solomon to the exile, note the historian's steady formula — each king named with the single word 'his son,' link after link — and they draw attention to one silent omission. Between Ahaziah and Joash the usurping queen Athaliah is passed over without mention, and rightly so, they explain, because she did not belong to the posterity of David; the register follows the blood of David, and the woman who broke into the throne by murder has no place in it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, as through the whole line, unlocks the names. Joram, or Jehoram, is 'Iahweh is high'; Ahaziah, 'Iah holdeth' — the same verb, he notes, that sounds in the song of Mary, where God 'hath holpen' his servant Israel in Luke 1:54; and Joash he takes to mean 'Iahweh is a hero.' The names carry the confession of the covenant God even through the reigns of kings who often betrayed him.

The witnesses find in the bare succession more than a list of crowns. That the line holds unbroken here, from Joram through Ahaziah to Joash, is itself a wonder, for these were among the darkest reigns of Judah, the throne all but extinguished when Athaliah destroyed the royal seed and only the infant Joash was hidden and preserved. Keil and Delitzsch's quiet note that Athaliah is left out because she was no child of David is, in effect, a confession that the covenant with David was never in her hand to break; the thread the historian traces survived her sword. So the register, naming three kings and passing over one queen, testifies that the promise to David's house ran on beneath the very reigns that most endangered it, kept not by the faithfulness of the kings but by the faithfulness of the God whose name their own names still spelled. Even here, in three words of succession, the covenant with David is quietly vindicated against every power that sought to break it.
3:
The Hebron list is completed by the fifth and sixth sons — Shephatiah of Abital, and Ithream by Eglah his wife — and the witnesses fasten almost entirely on that closing phrase, 'Eglah his wife,' and the old attempt to give the woman a famous name. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports that the Targum, Jarchi, and others maintained this Eglah was Michal, the daughter of Saul; but he objects that the identification does not well agree with 2 Sam. 6:23, where Michal is said to have had no child to the day of her death — though he concedes she might have borne one before the time there spoken of.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is blunter: Jewish expositors have groundlessly identified Eglah, whose name means 'heifer,' with Michal the daughter of Saul, named in 1 Sam. 18:20. Eglah, he observes, is not marked out as any principal wife of David; the words 'his wife' are added merely to balance the clause, supplying for the absence of the family details that are given for some of the other mothers. So the phrase that tempted the tradition is, on Ellicott's reading, nothing more than a scribe's way of rounding off the line.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes the same phrase in a gentler direction. Eglah may be called 'his wife,' he suggests, because she was David's first and therefore most proper wife, even though her son was born after all the rest already named, so that she and her son are set last, in the sixth place, the mother mentioned only for the son's sake. The disagreement is thus real though quiet: was Eglah a hidden name for Michal, Saul's barren daughter, or simply a wife distinguished by no more than the balancing words 'his wife'? The witnesses, weighing the childlessness of Michal against the plain sense of the text, incline as one away from the rabbinic guess, and leave Eglah as she stands — the mother of Ithream, the last of the six sons born to David in Hebron, honoured with a place in the record for her son's sake and no other.
8:
The tally of the Jerusalem sons closes at a number: 'and Elishama, and Eliada, and Eliphelet, nine.' The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke works the arithmetic that has puzzled readers of the parallels. There are thirteen sons if the four born to Bathsheba are counted in, and nine without them; the book of Samuel gives eleven with those four and only seven without. In Samuel, he reasons, probably only the sons then living were reckoned, while the Chronicler comprehends those also who had died — and he cites Jarchi, that the two duplicated names, Elishama and Eliphelet, are just what raise the seven to nine, the dead being numbered with the living because the whole book is written for the honour of David and his seed. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees: only seven are named in 2 Sam. 5:14, two being omitted there because they died very early, whereas here we have all the sons of David.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over one name in the closing three, Eliada, 'God knoweth,' which the parallel in 1 Chron. 14:7 gives as Beeliada, 'Lord knoweth.' The older form, he explains, is probably the more ancient, for God was of old called Baal — 'lord' — as well as El; and the title Baal was later discarded because it fostered confusion with the degrading worship of the Canaanite Baals. So men grew unwilling even to speak the word, and in names compounded with it substituted El or Iah, as here, or the word bosheth, 'shame,' as when Ishbosheth stands for Eshbaal.

The witnesses thus make the counting-verse a lesson in the historian's purpose and in the reverence of Israel's speech. Clarke and Wesley agree that the Chronicler numbers even the sons who died young, gathering the whole seed of David into the record, while Samuel counted only the living; the difference is not a contradiction but a difference of aim. And Ellicott's note on Eliada shows the very names changing over the centuries as Israel's jealousy for the one true God reshaped its tongue. Nine sons, then, are set down at Jerusalem — the full house of David's line, living and dead alike honoured with a place, that the seed from which the Christ would come might be counted entire.
22:
A small arithmetical snag halts the witnesses: 'the sons of Shemaiah; Hattush, and Igeal, and Bariah, and Neariah, and Shaphat, six' — yet only five names are given. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the problem baldly: five only are found in the text, and neither the versions nor the manuscripts supply the missing name; if the father Shemaiah is not himself to be counted among the six, then some name has dropped out of the record. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott concurs that the text as it stands gives five where it promises six, and takes it that one name has fallen away by an oversight of copying.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley offers two ways out. The number six may be reached by including the father Shemaiah together with his five sons; or else the Hebrew word rendered 'six,' Shisha, may not be a numeral at all but the proper name of one of the sons — so that the list in fact names its full complement, the last of them being called Shisha. The disagreement, then, is quiet but real: was a name lost, is the father counted in, or is 'six' itself a son's name?

Ellicott adds a note that lifts the verse out of mere counting. This Hattush, the firstborn of Shemaiah, is probably the very Hattush 'of the sons of David, of the sons of Shechaniah' whom Ezra 8:2-3 names among those who went up from Babylon in the second return, about 457 B.C.; and if the generations have been rightly counted, Hattush stands four descents after Zerubbabel and might well have been a youthful companion of Ezra. The witnesses thus turn even a scribe's slip to good account: the puzzle over 'six' shows how carefully the numbers were watched, and the identification of Hattush anchors this dim stretch of the genealogy to a known man of the return, a living descendant of David walking up from Babylon in Ezra's company. The royal line, obscure as it has grown, is still traceable in the flesh-and-blood names of the restoration. Even the missing sixth name, whether dropped by a copyist or hidden in the number itself, could not blot out the record that a son of David had come up again from Babylon.
24:
The register ends, and its last name carries the longest hope in the chapter: 'the sons of Elioenai... and Dalaiah, and Anani, seven.' The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the tradition that has gathered about this final Anani. 'This is the King Messiah who is to be revealed,' says the Targum, and Jarchi the same, both pointing to Dan. 7:13, 'Behold, one like the Son of man came with the clouds' — ananey — 'of heaven,' the very word, they fancy, hidden in the name Anani. Clarke himself thinks the ground of the application fanciful and not worth repeating, and he adds that the Syriac and Arabic drop several names from this table, making the chapter twenty-three verses only, such differences being frequent in Chronicles.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had seized on the same last name at the head of the royal roll. Bishop Patrick, he notes, records the Targum's gloss that Anani is the king Messiah who is to be revealed, and though Henry too calls the reasoning far-fetched, he takes it as evidence that the minds of the Jews were always full of the thought of the Messiah, and that they expected his coming not long after the days of Zerubbabel. So the chapter that opened in David's palace at Hebron closes with a name into which a whole nation had read its longing for the Son of David who was to come.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns from hope to chronology. These sons of Elioenai, he reckons, are the sixth generation from Zerubbabel, and so were living about 345 B.C., under Artaxerxes Ochus; and this, he notes, helps to fix the date of the whole book, for the register cannot have been closed before the men it last names were born. The witnesses thus leave the genealogy poised between measurement and mystery: Ellicott counting the generations down to a date late in the Persian age, and Clarke and Henry hearing, in the very last word of the list, the name the Targum turned into a promise. Whether or not Anani was ever meant to signify the Messiah, the instinct that read him so was sound, for the whole long descent — from Adam through David and the kings and the captives to these obscure returned families — was bending all the while toward the King who would come 'with the clouds of heaven,' in whom the line of David finds at last its meaning and its end.
4:
With the sixth son named, the historian pauses to cast up the account: these six were born to David in Hebron, where he reigned seven years and six months, before he reigned thirty and three years in Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the one witness to linger here, first attends to the wording. The Hebrew, he notes, says simply 'Six were born,' where the parallel at 2 Sam. 3:5 reads 'These were born' — a small difference of form in a verse otherwise shared between the two records.

His weightier observation concerns the chronological notice the Chronicler here inserts. This summary of the years David reigned, first in Hebron the Judean capital and then in Jerusalem over all Israel, is not read in the parallel section of Samuel at all; yet the same figures stand elsewhere in that book, at 2 Sam. 2:11 and 2 Sam. 5:5. The Chronicler, that is, has drawn the regnal reckoning from another place and set it here, at the seam between the Hebron sons and the Jerusalem sons, so that the list of children carries within it the frame of David's forty-year reign.

The verse, brief as it is, marks a turning of the whole passage. The two spans of years — the seven and a half at Hebron, the thirty-three at Jerusalem — measure the two stages of David's kingship, the narrower rule over Judah alone and the wider rule over the united nation from the city he made his own. Ellicott's care to trace each figure to its source in the older history shows the Chronicler doing what he does throughout, not inventing but gathering, setting down again for a returned and chastened people the foundation-facts of the throne on which all their hope of a coming king was built. The sons born in Hebron belong to the first of those stages; with the number totalled and the reign summed, the register is ready to cross over, as David himself crossed over, to Jerusalem, and to the sons who would be born to him there. The forty years thus divided — a span Scripture counts a full generation — stand as the settled foundation of the dynasty, the reign against which every later king of Judah would be measured, and to which the Chronicler's own hopeful readers looked back for the pledge of a throne that should not fail.
12:
Three more crowned names are added to the chain: 'Amaziah his son, Azariah his son, Jotham his son.' Only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott pauses over them, and again it is to read the theology folded into the names. Amaziah is 'Iah is strong'; Azariah, 'Iah helpeth'; Jotham, 'Iahweh is perfect' — three confessions of the LORD's strength, help, and perfection, borne by kings whose own reigns were a chequered mixture of obedience and failure.

The witnesses set these names within the larger reading the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had given of the whole list, that until Josiah the individual kings are named in strict order, each simply 'the son' of the one before, the descent passing without a break from father to son. And the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's reflection at the head of the line presides here too: it is the wonder of this genealogy that the crown ran so long in a direct line, seldom seen among the nations, and that this unbroken succession was the recompense of David's piety, God keeping his covenant with the father through generation after generation of sons.

So even a verse that is nothing but three names becomes, in the witnesses' hands, a testimony to covenant faithfulness. Azariah, the king better known as Uzziah, whose long and prosperous reign ended in the leprosy of presumption; Jotham his upright son, who did that which was right; and Amaziah before them, who began well and ended badly — all are gathered into the single formula 'his son,' the historian caring less to weigh each reign than to show the line preserved. The names themselves preach what the reigns so often denied: that the God of the house of David is strong to save, ready to help, and perfect in all his ways. The Chronicler numbers the three and moves on, each king a link by which the promise made to David was carried one generation nearer the King in whom it would at last be kept. Where the men faltered, the covenant held; and the register, indifferent to their greatness or their shame, records only that the sacred line ran on.
13:
The succession passes through a father, a son, and a grandson whose reigns could hardly have differed more: 'Ahaz his son, Hezekiah his son, Manasseh his son.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the lone commentator here, reads the names in his usual way. Ahaz he takes as an abbreviation of Jehoahaz, which is the same in sense as Ahaziah — 'Iah holdeth'; Hezekiah is Hizkiyahu, 'my strength is Iahu'; and of Manasseh he ventures only that the name is perhaps of Egyptian origin, a caution rather than a claim.

The witnesses let the three names stand in the frame the whole list has built. Here again the historian gives nothing but the succession, 'his son' after 'his son,' as the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch had said of the entire roll down to Josiah; and here the mere formula covers one of the sharpest swings in all the history of Judah — the faithless Ahaz who shut the temple doors, the faithful Hezekiah who reopened them, and Manasseh whose long idolatry all but undid his father's reform. Yet the register weighs none of this. It records only that the line held, that Ahaz begot Hezekiah and Hezekiah Manasseh, and that the covenant thread ran on through good king and bad alike.

In this the witnesses hear the genealogy's quiet doctrine. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's governing thought, that the unbroken descent was the recompense of David's piety, holds even over a wicked Ahaz and a Manasseh whose repentance came so late: the promise to David did not depend on the faithfulness of any single heir, but was carried forward by the God who had sworn it. The very name Hezekiah, 'my strength is the LORD,' set between two of Judah's most troubled reigns, speaks the reason the line survived them. The Chronicler names the three and hurries on, drawing the house of David one generation closer to the captivity, and beyond it to the King whom neither the sins of the fathers nor the exile itself could keep from coming. Three names, three utterly different reigns, and one unbroken promise — so the genealogy preaches, letting the plain formula 'his son' carry a weight no sermon could add.
14:
The line of anointed kings reaches its last unbroken link: 'Amon his son, Josiah his son.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses the two names — Amon, probably from the Egyptian sun-god Amen or Amun, and Josiah, 'Iah comforteth' — and then steps back to count the whole succession, noting that in this line of fifteen consecutive monarchs the usurper Athaliah is omitted between Ahaziah and Joash, exactly where the earlier verse had passed her by. The register, he observes, is a roll of David's descendants, and Athaliah was none.

The witnesses feel that with Josiah the chapter comes to a hinge. Down to this name the crown has descended in the direct line that the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry so admired, father to son through the long roll that was, he said, the recompense of David's piety; and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark that it is precisely with Josiah that the regular succession to the throne ceases. After him the historian will no longer be able to write simply 'his son,' but must number brothers and set down the broken order of the last kings, for the sons of Josiah did not follow one another by birthright but were set up and pulled down by the powers of Egypt and Babylon.

So the two quiet names close an era. Josiah, the great reformer, is the last king of Judah to hand his throne in peace to his own house; and the name that means 'the LORD comforteth' stands, fittingly, at the edge of the coming judgment, the last comfort before the storm. Ellicott's careful tally of the fifteen kings and his second reminder that Athaliah has no place among them keep the reader's eye on the integrity of the line — a line David's God had preserved through fifteen reigns and would not abandon even when, at the very next verse, its orderly descent gives way to the confusion of the exile. The Chronicler names Amon and Josiah, and pauses on the threshold of the captivity. Beyond them lie the reformer's death and the disorder his sons' names will bring; but here the line still stands whole, fifteen kings deep, a standing monument to the covenant that had carried it so far.
20:
Five further sons of Zerubbabel are numbered — 'Hashubah, and Ohel, and Berechiah, and Hasadiah, Jushab-hesed, five' — and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the sole witness, reads them as a second group of Zerubbabel's children, probably by another wife than the mother of the three named before. He notes a small textual point, that the connecting 'and' seems to have dropped out before the last name, Jushab-hesed, and then turns, with evident warmth, to what the names themselves declare.

For the names of Zerubbabel's kindred, Ellicott observes, breathe the religious hopefulness of the people at the dawn of the restoration, just as the names of the last kings had been dark parables of judgment — Shallum, 'recompense,' and Zedekiah, 'Iah is righteousness,' echoing the doom pronounced in Isa. 10:22, that 'a consumption is decreed, overflowing with righteousness.' The father's own name, Pedaiah, means 'Iah redeemeth'; his son Meshullam, 'devoted to God,' recalls the pious remnant so styled in Isa. 42:19; Ohel, 'tent,' is likely a shortening of a name pointing to the sacred dwelling of Jehovah, which was for ages a tent, as in Ezek. 37:27; and Jushab-hesed, most eloquent of all, means 'mercy shall be restored' — a very prophecy of faith in the God who, in wrath, remembers mercy, as Hab. 3:2 prays.

The witness thus lifts a bare row of five names into a confession of hope. Where the roll of the kings had run down into the names of judgment and captivity, the children of Zerubbabel are given names that look up — redemption, devotion, the tent of God's presence, and mercy restored. The returned remnant, naming their sons, wrote their theology into the family register, and Ellicott reads it out for us: a people newly home from Babylon, still under foreign rule, yet naming the next generation after the covenant mercies they were beginning again to taste. The genealogy, at this its dim and obscure end, quietly turns from lament to expectation, as the house of David, stripped of its crown, learns to hope in the God whose mercy would one day be restored to it in full.
21:
The line runs on past Zerubbabel's grandsons: 'the sons of Hananiah, Pelatiah and Jesaiah; the sons of Rephaiah, the sons of Arnan, the sons of Obadiah, the sons of Shechaniah.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds here an important variety of reading. The Hebrew as it stands lists Pelatiah — 'Iah is deliverance' — and Jesaiah, which is the very name Isaiah, 'Iah is salvation,' and then four families named after Rephaiah, Arnan, Obadiah, and Shechaniah, leaving their relation to Hananiah undefined. But the old versions, he shows, read differently: the Septuagint and Syriac turn the passage into a descending chain, 'Rephaiah his son, Arnan his son, Obadiah his son, Shechaniah his son,' the whole difference in Hebrew being only that between the letters for 'sons' and 'his son.'

Ellicott judges that this smoother reading is probably an ancient correction rather than the original, since the received text, awkward as it is, is the harder and likely the truer; and he concludes that 'the sons of Hananiah' is meant to gather in not only Pelatiah and Jesaiah but also the four families that follow, whether these were brothers of the first two or subdivisions descended from them. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads it to the same effect: all of them, parents and sons together, are set down as the sons of Hananiah and branches of the royal stock.

The witnesses value the verse for the glimpse it gives of the historian's fidelity to a difficult text. Rather than smooth the record into an easy line of succession, the Chronicler — or the tradition he received — kept the tangled form, and Ellicott, weighing manuscript against manuscript, prefers to leave the knot rather than cut it. The names themselves, Pelatiah 'the LORD delivers' and Jesaiah 'the LORD saves,' still sound the note of hope that had marked Zerubbabel's house; and the four shadowy families that hang from Hananiah's name show the royal stock spreading, generation by obscure generation, through the long silence after the return, the blood of David preserved in families whose only monument is this list. The witnesses read even the textual difficulty as a testimony that the register was copied with reverent care, its very roughness a mark of honesty.
23:
The generations thin toward the chapter's end: 'the sons of Neariah; Elioenai, and Hezekiah, and Azrikam, three.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, alone attending the verse, dwells on the first and fullest of the names. Elioenai, he explains, means 'unto Iah are mine eyes' — an expansion of the same devout thought that the psalmist breathes when he lifts up his eyes to the God enthroned in the heavens, as in Ps. 123:1-2 and again in Ps. 25:15, 'mine eyes are ever toward the LORD.' The name is a prayer, a whole posture of waiting hope folded into a single word.

Ellicott notes, too, that an Elioenai went up with Ezra from Babylon, as Ezra 8:4 records — another of the descendants of David whom the register here names and whom the history of the return names again, so that the dim genealogy and the living record of the restoration touch at a point. The line has grown obscure, its names no longer those of kings but of quiet families in a subject province; yet it is not lost, and here and there, as in Elioenai, a name can still be matched to a man who came home.

The witnesses read the verse in the spirit that has governed the whole close of the chapter. Where the roll of the kings had ended in judgment and exile, the post-exilic names look upward and forward — Elioenai, 'my eyes are unto the LORD,' set among the grandchildren of Zerubbabel, is of a piece with the Jushab-hesed and Meshullam of the verses before, the theology of a returned and waiting people written into their children's names. The house of David, stripped of its throne and scattered by Babylon, still lifts its eyes to the covenant God; and the historian, faithfully numbering three obscure sons of Neariah, shows that the line was preserved, and that its hope was not in any crown it still wore but in the LORD toward whom its very names were turned. One more generation, and the chapter, and the register, will reach its last and most freighted name.
